learning_outcomes.description: Analyse personal and organisational situations from multiple perspectives.
Work effectively in a team towards the presentation of ideas, analysis and conclusions.
Engage in structured reflection as a way to leverage personal learning.
Design an effective change management plan.

assessments.due_date:
Presentation Slides - 1PM - - Week 8 Mon

Presentation during class Week 8 Mon - Week 9 Mon

Presentation made during class in Weeks 8 and 9 - Powerpoint slides for all groups presenting in Weeks 8 and 9 to be submitted via Blackboard by the Week 8 due date.
assessments.task_description:
Assessment 1: Four frames group presentation (20 marks)

Your team of 6 (maximum size) will deliver a live in class 15 minute presentation with all group members present (with a 3-5 minute section of Questions and Answers (Q and A) - so 20 minutes in total) to the whole class on a challenging problem that allows a detailed four frames analysis. Groups that present on the same day will lead the Q and A session.

The Four Frames topics will be identified in weeks 1 and 2 of the course and time will be given during the course to work on these topics. Topics should be selected that are: (1) topical; (2) reflect some of the criteria of a “wicked problem”; (3) it will be possible to find quality academic research articles or industry (grey) reports about the issue and (4) the topic opens itself to the application of all of the four frames. All groups will need to have sign off from the lecturer by the end of Week 3 lecture that their topic is approved.

Special attention by each member of the group should be given to the incorporation of the academic literature (will be 80% or more of the Reference List) to support key points being made under each frame; smooth transitions between speakers and the frames are expected; and presenting in the time frame allocated of 15 minutes plus 3-5 minutes of questions. See the marking rubric for more guidance.

There will be a maximum of 12 PowerPoint slides used in the presentation (plus not part of the 12 slides maximum, 2 extra slides, one introductory slide that gives the topic name and team members’ names; and 2-3 slides of all of the references used in preparing the presentation, and the completed AI proforma as the final slide.

Each of the 6 team members will present as the assessment mark will be based on the quality of the slides, and each individual's contribution to the presentation and the Q and A section. Prior examples of successful presentations are provided in Week 1 and on the Blackboard site.

Peer Review: Group members are expected to contribute equally, fulfilling the tasks agreed on. If there are disagreements in relation to team members' contributions that cannot be resolved, students must bring the problem to the attention of the lecturer as early as possible. Students will undertake then a Peer Review process, which can influence the marks of those who do not contribute to group work.

Please Note: The presentation will be recorded for marking purposes.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Assessment 2: Four frames individual report (40% of the final grade)

2000 words (excluding Reference List)

Task Description:

The primary purpose of this assignment is to develop your skills and understanding in applying the four organisational frames, as well as other relevant models and concepts from the course, to analyse and solve organisational, community or societal problems. The assignment will also further develop your written communication and analytical skills as an industry consultant. You should approach the report as a consultancy project that could be delivered to a client.

The Task: Use the four (three frames is also permissible, but not less) organisational frames and related concepts and literature to identify, analyse and offer a set of recommendations to one of the following five wicked or complex challenges.

1.   Healthcare Inequities and System Strain

  • Structure: For example - Fragmented public-private payer systems and clinic networks.
  • Human Resources: e.g., Severe healthcare worker burnout and staffing shortages.
  • Power: e.g., Lobbying power of pharmaceuticals vs. patient advocacy groups.
  • Culture: e.g., Conflicting societal values regarding the "right" to healthcare.

2.   Workplace Resistance to Artificial Intelligence

  • Structure: e.g., Obsolete organizational charts and workflows that do not account for AI augmentation.
  • Human Resources: e.g., Employee fear of obsolescence and the need for rapid, massive reskilling.
  • Power: e.g., Tension between tech-forward leadership and threatened legacy departments.
  • Culture: e.g., Deeply held beliefs about the morality of human-led labour vs. automated production.

3.   Climate Change and Corporate Sustainability

  • Structure: e.g., Inadequate global governance frameworks and supply chain emissions.
  • Human Resources: e.g., Eco-anxiety among the workforce and difficulty retaining younger, mission-driven talent.
  • Power: e.g., Competing interests of fossil fuel lobbies, government regulators, and climate activists.
  • Culture: e.g., Societal and consumer addiction to hyper-consumption and convenience.

4.   Remote/Hybrid Work and Dispersed Culture

  • Structure: e.g., Lack of standardised metrics for measuring productivity outside the traditional office.
  • Human Resources: e.g., Frayed employee mental health, loneliness, and uneven work-life boundaries.
  • Power: e.g., Clashes over control (micromanagement) vs. autonomy (employee desire for remote flexibility).
  • Culture: e.g., The struggle to build shared organisational identity and spontaneous collaboration ("watercooler moments").

5.   Organizational Knowledge Retention and The Aging Workforce

  • Structure: e.g., Lack of formal knowledge management systems and succession planning processes.
  • Human Resources: e.g., Generational clashes between baby boomers, Gen X, and Gen Z, alongside massive brain drain.
  • Power: e.g., Senior employees hoarding knowledge to maintain job security vs. younger employees wanting upward mobility.
  • Culture: e.g., A culture that fails to value knowledge sharing, mentorship, and continuous learning.

-

You should structure the report as follows:

  • 1.0 Brief overview - Briefly describe the issue and the organisational or community context clearly, including its degree of “wickedness’.
  • 2.0 Critical analysis - Critically analyse and evaluate the issue from the perspective of each of the four (or three) organisational frames: use the four subheadings of 2.1 Structural, 2.2 Human resource, 2.3 Political and 2.4 Cultural. Support your analysis and demonstrate your insight into your issue by integrating relevant academic literature, ideally peer reviewed quality journal articles. This is a critical criterion. Consider how you can support your claims and key points by using this quality academic research.
  • 3.0 Recommendations - Draw on your knowledge of the frames used to make specific recommendations for how the issue or problem could have been more effectively addressed or resolved.

This report must be heavily referenced in the application of quality peer reviewed academic literature, in particular academic journals, in making your critical examination of the key issues. Appendix A placed before the reference list must provide a summary list of all sources used showing the Australian Business Deans Council (ABDC) Journal Source by journal name, frequency of articles from this journal and ABDC journal ranking. Must total at least 70% of all sources used are from this ABDC journal list, and this Appendix A needs to report this calculated percentage. No need to report a summary of non-ABDC sources that will be no more than 30% of all sources used to complete the assignment. 

Also the following must apply:

  • Abstracts/executive summaries are not required and should not be included.
  • Use a standard referencing system, to provide a totally accurate and complete list of all references used in compiling the report. The reference list is not included in the word count. Digital Object Identifier (DOIs) must be included for any journal article that is in the reference list.
  • Assignments exceeding the word limit by more than 10% will be penalised in line with UQ policy. 
  • You can assume complete confidentiality: The report is for educational purposes only and will be treated as confidential. 
  • Marking will follow the rubric provided on the course Blackboard site. Look again at the rubric as you prepare the report.
  • A word count must be provided on the cover page with your name and student number.
  • Also on the cover page you must report on the title of your Group Presentation - Group Presentation topics and the Individual Essay topics must NOT overlap to any great degree
  • Appendix A (ABDC list and summary) must be included
  • Appendix B (AI statement) must be attached to the submission as the final page
  • DOIs must be included in all references. A random check will be made of the Reference List to determine its validity via links to the DOIs.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Assessment 3: Change management case study examination
assessments.task_description:
Assessment 3: Change Management Case Study Examination - Central Examination Period

Task Description:

A focus on the final weeks of the course is upon developing your change management knowledge and skills. It is these analytical skills around change that will be an asset to you initially in your career, either in analysing your own emerging roles and your changing organisation, or those of a client. The final examination will assess these analytical skills.

Your task as a set of examination questions will be to provide as a business consultant a consultancy report in relation to a case study that will be provided as the basis of the examination. Your tasks will include, for example (1) initially describing the nature of this change around its degree of key drivers, complexity, timing and consequences; (2) how this change should be managed and lead either across a number of key stages or in terms of addressing a number of major issues that are typically identified in the academic literature and in numerous change management models or frameworks, as essential for successful change; and (3) finally as a consultant you will provide a list of recommendations that follow on from this detailed critical analysis based on this critical analysis applying academic literature and analysis.

Your sources of data that will guide your analysis of the change, and the list of recommendations, must include the major use of the large body of academic research addressed in the course seminars, and other published materials in reputable academic journals as articles, as book chapters, quality books and possibly reputable web pages (e.g., of management consulting companies).

In sum, in preparation for this Change Management Case Study Examination, you will need to be able to use and apply in a critical manner the key ideas and findings addressed in the course seminars, and from scholarly articles from quality sources, to provide: 1) an analysis of the key issues that might be in play according to studies of best practice in the change process (including change drivers, why these issues exist, what can be done to resolve them); 2) using your knowledge of the change management literature, potential options available to the leaders and managers, and their likely effectiveness, in your analysis of the case study; and 3) following on from 1 and 2, to provide a number (3 or more) of recommendations about how this change might be effectively planned and executed.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to MGTS4602 and Using framing to examine wicked problems

Introduction to Leadership and Strategy Practicum (MGTS4602), including course overview, objectives, learning materials and resources, and assessment; introduction to different degrees of wicked problems, and the role of the change agent/consultant, and the notion of framing and reframing towards seeking strategies to resolve problems

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Introduction to MGTS4602 and Using framing to examine wicked problems

learning_activities.topic:
Leadership and strategy overview

Using framing - Overview of the four frames, the case of workplace gender equity as a wicked problem, and introduction to the structural frame.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Leadership and strategy overview Using framing - Overview of the four frames, the case of workplace gender equity as a wicked problem, and introduction to the structural frame.

learning_activities.topic:
The structural frame

Analysis using the structural frame that considers the role of getting organised, appropriate structures and restructuring, and organising groups and teams in identifying strategies to solving challenging problems

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: The structural frame Analysis using the structural frame that considers the role of getting organised, appropriate structures and restructuring, and organising groups and teams in identifying strategies to solving challenging problems

learning_activities.topic:
The human resource frame

Under the human resource frame we will investigate the roles of people and organisations, improving human resource management and the contribution of groups and teams in helping leaders solve challenging and wicked problems especially around aspects of people management

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: The human resource frame Under the human resource frame we will investigate the roles of people and organisations, improving human resource management and the contribution of groups and teams in helping leaders solve challenging and wicked problems especially around aspects of people management

learning_activities.topic:
The human resource frame (Continued)

Continue the examination of how groups and teams can be used as valuable methods to explore strategies and solutions to challenging and wicked problems

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: The human resource frame (Continued) Continue the examination of how groups and teams can be used as valuable methods to explore strategies and solutions to challenging and wicked problems

learning_activities.topic:
The political frame

Organisations and communities often have political agendas that impact upon the ability to resolve a challenging problem and we will begin to investigate to roles of power, conflict, coalitions, and the manager as change agent, politician and political agent

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: The political frame Organisations and communities often have political agendas that impact upon the ability to resolve a challenging problem and we will begin to investigate to roles of power, conflict, coalitions, and the manager as change agent, politician and political agent

learning_activities.topic:
Symbolic or cultural framing

Complete the analysis of thinking about organisational and community challenges from the political frame of power and politics and begin to investigate the value in seeing how changes to issues also have symbolic and cultural issues that need to be highlighted and managed if changes are to occur

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Groups will continue to present their four frame analysis of an organisational or community problem, addressing each frame and putting forward strategies to resolve these wicked types of problems

learning_activities.topic:
Management and leadership of change

Introduce an examination of different ideas and frameworks about how to lead change that builds the engagement of people and communities in developing strategies that promote more successful change, including both incremental and more radical change

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Management and leadership of change

Continue the discussion of how to lead change, including the more effective models and strategies to engage people and communities to own the changes being implemented. We will review the course, the value of framing and reframing, and discuss the progress on the change management assignment

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

We will review the course, the value of framing and reframing, and discuss the progress on the change management assignment

learning_activities.topic:
Management and leadership of change (conclusion), revision; and examination briefing

Finalise the discussion of how to lead change, including the more effective models and strategies to engage people and communities to own the changes being implemented. We will continue to review the course, the value of framing and reframing, and discuss the progress on the change management assignment, and do personal reflections on what has been learned
Readings/Ref: Bolman & Deal (Chapters 18-20);

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
